BRD Document Content:
Business Requirements Document (BRD)
Loan Banking Data Mart & Reporting Initiative
Version 1.0
1. Document Purpose
This BRD defines the business requirements for designing and implementing a loan banking data mart and reporting solution. The document consolidates business needs captured in the provided workbook and translates them into a formal narrative format suitable for project delivery, stakeholder sign-off, and implementation planning.
2. Business Background
Loan banking functions typically operate across multiple systems for origination, servicing, collections, collateral management, and finance. Fragmented data creates delays in reporting, inconsistent metrics, and manual reconciliation effort. A unified analytical layer is required to improve visibility across the lending lifecycle.
3. Business Objectives
The target state is to create a governed and scalable data foundation that enables accurate portfolio reporting, risk monitoring, collections prioritization, recovery tracking, and management decision-making.
4. Scope
In scope are loan accounts, customer relationships, balances, repayment schedules, delinquency indicators, collateral details, guarantor information, provisions, recoveries, and associated reference data. Out of scope are changes to core transaction processing systems and front-office workflow redesign unless specifically approved.
5. Stakeholders
Primary stakeholders include Lending Operations, Credit Risk, Collections, Finance, Regulatory Reporting, Data Engineering, BI/Analytics teams, Compliance, and Technology Support.
6. Current State Challenges
Current reporting processes rely on multiple extracts and manual spreadsheets. Definitions of KPIs may vary across teams. Data lineage is unclear for several metrics. Turnaround time for ad hoc analysis is high, and reconciliation cycles delay management reporting.
7. Future State Requirements
The future solution shall provide curated data marts, standardized calculations, reusable dimensions, documented business rules, historical trending capability, and controlled access for business consumers.
8. Functional Requirements
System shall ingest data from all approved source tables identified in the workbook.
System shall standardize business keys for customer, loan, facility, collateral, and branch entities.
System shall apply transformation logic and derivations captured in column logic/reference sheets.
System shall generate subject-area data marts for portfolio, delinquency, collateral, provisioning, and recovery reporting.
System shall maintain historical snapshots where periodic trend analysis is required.
System shall support drill-down from summary KPIs to underlying account-level records.
System shall provide metadata definitions for each published attribute.
System shall support scheduled refresh cycles and controlled reruns.
9. Reporting Requirements
Outstanding portfolio by product, geography, segment, and branch.
Past-due balances by aging bucket and movement trends.
High-risk exposure and concentration dashboards.
Collateral coverage ratios and unsecured exposure views.
Provision balances and movement analysis.
Recovery collections by channel, period, and product.
Executive scorecards with monthly and quarterly trends.
10. Data Requirements
The workbook references multiple sheets containing business summaries, datamart overviews, column logic, and loan table column references. These inputs shall be treated as authoritative sources for data element definitions, source mappings, and calculation logic during design and build phases.
11. Business Rules
Latest status-based attributes shall use the most recent effective date.
Amounts shall be aggregated at the agreed reporting grain.
Null or invalid keys shall be quarantined for remediation.
Derived flags and buckets shall follow approved business definitions.
Historical records shall be retained according to retention policy.
12. Non-Functional Requirements
Performance: Standard reports should meet agreed SLA timings.
Scalability: Architecture should support growth in loan volume and new products.
Security: Role-based access control and masking for sensitive fields.
Auditability: Full lineage from report field to source system.
Availability: Data marts available as per business calendar.
Quality: Reconciliation, completeness, duplicate, and validity checks.
13. Assumptions & Dependencies
Source systems will provide timely extracts. Business SMEs will validate definitions and exceptions. Required infrastructure, connectivity, and access approvals will be available within project timelines.
14. Risks
Risks include source data quality issues, evolving regulatory definitions, delayed sign-offs, and upstream schema changes. These shall be managed through governance and change control.
15. Acceptance Criteria
Approved in-scope data sources are integrated.
KPIs reconcile with legacy reports within agreed tolerance.
Business users sign off on critical reports and definitions.
Security controls are tested and approved.
Operational support documentation is completed.
Conceptual draft generated. Review the conceptual ER and use the same /orchestrate endpoint with artifact_id to update relationships or send 'approve' to continue.
Conceptual draft approved and used to generate logical and physical models.

conceptual.rag_context_used:
Business concept: Customer. Canonical table: CUSTOMER. Definition: A borrower or counterparty who receives credit from the institution. Business purpose: Represents the primary party against whom credit exposure is established. Notes: One customer can have one or more facilities.
Business concept: Facility. Canonical table: FACILITY. Definition: A sanctioned credit arrangement approved for a customer, such as a term loan, overdraft, or working capital line. Business purpose: Acts as the contractual credit agreement and parent container for one or more loans or drawdowns. Notes: Central entity in the loan model.
Business concept: Loan. Canonical table: LOAN. Definition: An individual drawdown or funded exposure created under a facility. Business purpose: Tracks the specific credit amount disbursed, pricing, and lifecycle status. Notes: A facility can generate one or more loans.
Business concept: Collateral. Canonical table: COLLATERAL. Definition: An asset pledged as security against credit exposure. Business purpose: Mitigates credit risk by providing secondary repayment support. Notes: Examples include property, receivables, inventory, vehicles, and deposits.
Business concept: Facility Collateral. Canonical table: FACILITY_COLLATERAL. Definition: The bridge entity linking a facility to one or more collateral records. Business purpose: Resolves the many-to-many style business association between secured facilities and collateral items. Notes: Also stores lien order in this model.
Business concept: Guarantor. Canonical table: GUARANTOR. Definition: A third party that legally supports repayment if the borrower fails to perform. Business purpose: Provides additional credit enhancement beyond the borrower and collateral. Notes: Can be an individual or a corporate guarantor.
Business concept: Provision. Canonical table: PROVISION. Definition: A period-end expected or recognized credit loss amount for a facility. Business purpose: Supports accounting and risk reporting by recording loss allowance over time. Notes: Includes stage-based provision amounts in this model.
Business concept: Stage. Canonical table: PROVISION. Definition: A credit impairment classification used for provisioning. Business purpose: Distinguishes performing, underperforming, and credit-impaired exposures for loss estimation. Notes: This synthetic model includes Stage 1, Stage 2, and Stage 3 values.
Business concept: Sanctioned Amount. Canonical table: FACILITY. Definition: The approved credit limit or facility size granted to the customer. Business purpose: Defines the maximum contractual exposure approved under the facility. Notes: A loan amount may be lower than the sanctioned amount.
Table summary: CUSTOMER represents Customer. Primary key: customer_id. Business attributes: customer_name means display name of the borrower or counterparty; customer_type means segment of the customer such as Retail, SME, or Corporate; date_of_birth means date of birth of the customer. Examples: customer_id example C00001; customer_name example Aarav Sharma; customer_type example Retail.
Table summary: FACILITY represents Facility. Related business terms: Sanctioned Amount. Primary key: facility_id. Foreign keys: customer_id references CUSTOMER.customer_id. Business attributes: facility_type means type of sanctioned credit arrangement; sanctioned_amount means approved amount or limit for the facility; start_date means facility effective or start date; maturity_date means scheduled end or maturity date of the facility. Examples: facility_id example F00001; customer_id example C00001; facility_type example Term Loan.
Table summary: LOAN represents Loan. Primary key: loan_id. Foreign keys: facility_id references FACILITY.facility_id. Business attributes: loan_amount means amount disbursed for the individual loan; interest_rate means contractual pricing rate associated with the loan; disbursement_date means date on which the loan amount was funded or made available; loan_status means lifecycle status of the loan. Examples: loan_id example L00001; facility_id example F00001; loan_amount example 650000.
Table summary: COLLATERAL represents Collateral. Primary key: collateral_id. Business attributes: collateral_type means type of pledged security; collateral_value means assessed value of the collateral at valuation date; valuation_date means date on which the collateral was valued. Examples: collateral_id example CL00001; collateral_type example Property; collateral_value example 1500000.
Table summary: FACILITY_COLLATERAL represents Facility Collateral. Primary key: facility_collateral_id. Foreign keys: facility_id references FACILITY.facility_id; collateral_id references COLLATERAL.collateral_id. Business attributes: lien_order means priority of the institution's charge over the collateral. Examples: facility_collateral_id example FC00001; facility_id example F00001; collateral_id example CL00001.
Table summary: GUARANTOR represents Guarantor. Primary key: guarantor_id. Business attributes: guarantor_name means name of the guarantor party; guarantor_type means type of guarantor such as Individual or Corporate. Examples: guarantor_id example G00001; guarantor_name example Priya Mehta; guarantor_type example Individual.
Table summary: PROVISION represents Provision. Related business terms: Stage. Primary key: provision_id. Foreign keys: facility_id references FACILITY.facility_id. Business attributes: provision_date means reporting date for the provision amount; stage means impairment stage associated with the provision record; provision_amount means recognized or expected credit loss amount for the facility at provision date. Examples: provision_id example P000001; facility_id example F00001; provision_date example 2025-12-31.

relationships.description: A customer can have one or more facilities.
relationships.label: Customer has Facility

relationships.description: A facility can generate one or more loans.
relationships.label: Facility funds Loan

relationships.description: A facility can be associated with one or more facility-collateral link records.
relationships.label: Facility linked by Facility Collateral

relationships.description: A collateral item can participate in one or more facility-collateral link records.
relationships.label: Collateral linked by Facility Collateral

relationships.description: A facility may be supported by one or more guarantors, and a guarantor may support multiple facilities.
relationships.label: Facility supported by Guarantor

relationships.description: A facility can have one or more period-end provision records over time.
relationships.label: Facility has Provision

business_rules: One customer can have one or more facilities.
A facility is the contractual credit agreement and parent container for one or more loans or drawdowns.
A loan is an individual funded exposure created under a facility.
A facility can be associated with multiple collateral records through the facility-collateral bridge concept.
Facility collateral also stores lien order in this model.
A guarantor is a third party that supports repayment if the borrower fails to perform.
A provision is recorded at period end for a facility and includes stage-based provision amounts.
Stage 1, Stage 2, and Stage 3 are the supported impairment stages for provisioning.
conceptual.conceptual_summary: The model centers on Customer, Facility, and Loan as the lending core, with Facility acting as the sanctioned credit arrangement and parent of funded Loan exposures. Credit risk mitigation is represented through Collateral and the Facility Collateral bridge, while Guarantor captures additional repayment support. Provision provides period-end loss allowance reporting by facility and stage. All entities are directly connected through business relationships supported by the glossary.
conceptual.diagram_description: Customer sits upstream of Facility in a one-to-many relationship. Facility branches to Loan in a one-to-many relationship, to Provision in a one-to-many relationship, and to Guarantor in a many-to-many support relationship. Collateral connects to Facility through the Facility Collateral bridge, which resolves the many-to-many association and carries lien order. The overall shape is a lending core with surrounding credit enhancement and accounting concepts.

relationships.description: A customer can have one or more facilities.
A facility can generate one or more loans.
A facility can be associated with one or more facility-collateral link records.
A collateral item can participate in one or more facility-collateral link records.
A facility may be supported by one or more guarantors, and a guarantor may support multiple facilities. Resolved through Facility_Guarantor.
A facility can have one or more period-end provision records over time.

normalization_notes: All source concepts are represented at the logical table level with numeric surrogate primary keys.
The M:N relationship between Facility and Guarantor is resolved using the associative table Facility_Guarantor.
The Facility Collateral bridge concept is modeled as its own associative table with lien_order captured as an attribute.
All foreign keys use the same numeric type as their referenced surrogate keys.
Stage values for Provision are expected to follow the approved conceptual model: Stage 1, Stage 2, and Stage 3.
No physical storage, indexing, partitioning, or implementation-specific details are included.

ddl:
CREATE TABLE Customer (
  Customer_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  customer_name VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,
  customer_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  date_of_birth DATE NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Customer PRIMARY KEY (Customer_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Facility (
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Customer_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  facility_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  sanctioned_amount DECIMAL(18,2) NOT NULL,
  start_date DATE NOT NULL,
  maturity_date DATE NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Facility PRIMARY KEY (Facility_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Facility_Customer_ID FOREIGN KEY (Customer_ID) REFERENCES Customer (Customer_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Loan (
  Loan_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  loan_amount DECIMAL(18,2) NOT NULL,
  interest_rate DECIMAL(9,4) NOT NULL,
  disbursement_date DATE NOT NULL,
  loan_status VARCHAR(30) NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Loan PRIMARY KEY (Loan_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Loan_Facility_ID FOREIGN KEY (Facility_ID) REFERENCES Facility (Facility_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Collateral (
  Collateral_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  collateral_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  collateral_value DECIMAL(18,2) NOT NULL,
  valuation_date DATE NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Collateral PRIMARY KEY (Collateral_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Facility_Collateral (
  Facility_Collateral_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Collateral_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  lien_order INTEGER NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Facility_Collateral PRIMARY KEY (Facility_Collateral_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Facility_Collateral_Facility_ID FOREIGN KEY (Facility_ID) REFERENCES Facility (Facility_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Facility_Collateral_Collateral_ID FOREIGN KEY (Collateral_ID) REFERENCES Collateral (Collateral_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Facility_Guarantor (
  Facility_Guarantor_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Guarantor_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Facility_Guarantor PRIMARY KEY (Facility_Guarantor_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Facility_Guarantor_Facility_ID FOREIGN KEY (Facility_ID) REFERENCES Facility (Facility_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Facility_Guarantor_Guarantor_ID FOREIGN KEY (Guarantor_ID) REFERENCES Guarantor (Guarantor_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Provision (
  Provision_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  provision_date DATE NOT NULL,
  stage VARCHAR(30) NOT NULL,
  provision_amount DECIMAL(18,2) NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Provision PRIMARY KEY (Provision_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Provision_Facility_ID FOREIGN KEY (Facility_ID) REFERENCES Facility (Facility_ID)
);

Business concept: Customer. Canonical table: CUSTOMER. Definition: A borrower or counterparty who receives credit from the institution. Business purpose: Represents the primary party against whom credit exposure is established. Notes: One customer can have one or more facilities.
Business concept: Facility. Canonical table: FACILITY. Definition: A sanctioned credit arrangement approved for a customer, such as a term loan, overdraft, or working capital line. Business purpose: Acts as the contractual credit agreement and parent container for one or more loans or drawdowns. Notes: Central entity in the loan model.
Business concept: Loan. Canonical table: LOAN. Definition: An individual drawdown or funded exposure created under a facility. Business purpose: Tracks the specific credit amount disbursed, pricing, and lifecycle status. Notes: A facility can generate one or more loans.
Business concept: Collateral. Canonical table: COLLATERAL. Definition: An asset pledged as security against credit exposure. Business purpose: Mitigates credit risk by providing secondary repayment support. Notes: Examples include property, receivables, inventory, vehicles, and deposits.
Business concept: Facility Collateral. Canonical table: FACILITY_COLLATERAL. Definition: The bridge entity linking a facility to one or more collateral records. Business purpose: Resolves the many-to-many style business association between secured facilities and collateral items. Notes: Also stores lien order in this model.
Business concept: Guarantor. Canonical table: GUARANTOR. Definition: A third party that legally supports repayment if the borrower fails to perform. Business purpose: Provides additional credit enhancement beyond the borrower and collateral. Notes: Can be an individual or a corporate guarantor.
Business concept: Provision. Canonical table: PROVISION. Definition: A period-end expected or recognized credit loss amount for a facility. Business purpose: Supports accounting and risk reporting by recording loss allowance over time. Notes: Includes stage-based provision amounts in this model.
Business concept: Stage. Canonical table: PROVISION. Definition: A credit impairment classification used for provisioning. Business purpose: Distinguishes performing, underperforming, and credit-impaired exposures for loss estimation. Notes: This synthetic model includes Stage 1, Stage 2, and Stage 3 values.
Business concept: Sanctioned Amount. Canonical table: FACILITY. Definition: The approved credit limit or facility size granted to the customer. Business purpose: Defines the maximum contractual exposure approved under the facility. Notes: A loan amount may be lower than the sanctioned amount.
Table summary: CUSTOMER represents Customer. Primary key: customer_id. Business attributes: customer_name means display name of the borrower or counterparty; customer_type means segment of the customer such as Retail, SME, or Corporate; date_of_birth means date of birth of the customer. Examples: customer_id example C00001; customer_name example Aarav Sharma; customer_type example Retail.
Table summary: FACILITY represents Facility. Related business terms: Sanctioned Amount. Primary key: facility_id. Foreign keys: customer_id references CUSTOMER.customer_id. Business attributes: facility_type means type of sanctioned credit arrangement; sanctioned_amount means approved amount or limit for the facility; start_date means facility effective or start date; maturity_date means scheduled end or maturity date of the facility. Examples: facility_id example F00001; customer_id example C00001; facility_type example Term Loan.
Table summary: LOAN represents Loan. Primary key: loan_id. Foreign keys: facility_id references FACILITY.facility_id. Business attributes: loan_amount means amount disbursed for the individual loan; interest_rate means contractual pricing rate associated with the loan; disbursement_date means date on which the loan amount was funded or made available; loan_status means lifecycle status of the loan. Examples: loan_id example L00001; facility_id example F00001; loan_amount example 650000.
Table summary: COLLATERAL represents Collateral. Primary key: collateral_id. Business attributes: collateral_type means type of pledged security; collateral_value means assessed value of the collateral at valuation date; valuation_date means date on which the collateral was valued. Examples: collateral_id example CL00001; collateral_type example Property; collateral_value example 1500000.
Table summary: FACILITY_COLLATERAL represents Facility Collateral. Primary key: facility_collateral_id. Foreign keys: facility_id references FACILITY.facility_id; collateral_id references COLLATERAL.collateral_id. Business attributes: lien_order means priority of the institution's charge over the collateral. Examples: facility_collateral_id example FC00001; facility_id example F00001; collateral_id example CL00001.
Table summary: GUARANTOR represents Guarantor. Primary key: guarantor_id. Business attributes: guarantor_name means name of the guarantor party; guarantor_type means type of guarantor such as Individual or Corporate. Examples: guarantor_id example G00001; guarantor_name example Priya Mehta; guarantor_type example Individual.
Table summary: PROVISION represents Provision. Related business terms: Stage. Primary key: provision_id. Foreign keys: facility_id references FACILITY.facility_id. Business attributes: provision_date means reporting date for the provision amount; stage means impairment stage associated with the provision record; provision_amount means recognized or expected credit loss amount for the facility at provision date. Examples: provision_id example P000001; facility_id example F00001; provision_date example 2025-12-31.

business_rules: One customer can have one or more facilities.
A facility is the contractual credit agreement and parent container for one or more loans or drawdowns.
A loan is an individual funded exposure created under a facility.
A facility can be associated with multiple collateral records through the facility-collateral bridge concept.
Facility collateral also stores lien order in this model.
A guarantor is a third party that supports repayment if the borrower fails to perform.
A provision is recorded at period end for a facility and includes stage-based provision amounts.
Stage 1, Stage 2, and Stage 3 are the supported impairment stages for provisioning.
model_json.conceptual_summary: The model centers on Customer, Facility, and Loan as the lending core, with Facility acting as the sanctioned credit arrangement and parent of funded Loan exposures. Credit risk mitigation is represented through Collateral and the Facility Collateral bridge, while Guarantor captures additional repayment support. Provision provides period-end loss allowance reporting by facility and stage. All entities are directly connected through business relationships supported by the glossary.
model_json.diagram_description: Customer sits upstream of Facility in a one-to-many relationship. Facility branches to Loan in a one-to-many relationship, to Provision in a one-to-many relationship, and to Guarantor in a many-to-many support relationship. Collateral connects to Facility through the Facility Collateral bridge, which resolves the many-to-many association and carries lien order. The overall shape is a lending core with surrounding credit enhancement and accounting concepts.

relationships.description: A customer can have one or more facilities.
A facility can generate one or more loans.
A facility can be associated with one or more facility-collateral link records.
A collateral item can participate in one or more facility-collateral link records.
A facility may be supported by one or more guarantors, and a guarantor may support multiple facilities. Resolved through Facility_Guarantor.
A facility can have one or more period-end provision records over time.

normalization_notes: All source concepts are represented at the logical table level with numeric surrogate primary keys.
The M:N relationship between Facility and Guarantor is resolved using the associative table Facility_Guarantor.
The Facility Collateral bridge concept is modeled as its own associative table with lien_order captured as an attribute.
All foreign keys use the same numeric type as their referenced surrogate keys.
Stage values for Provision are expected to follow the approved conceptual model: Stage 1, Stage 2, and Stage 3.
No physical storage, indexing, partitioning, or implementation-specific details are included.

CREATE TABLE Customer (
  Customer_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  customer_name VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,
  customer_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  date_of_birth DATE NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Customer PRIMARY KEY (Customer_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Facility (
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Customer_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  facility_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  sanctioned_amount DECIMAL(18,2) NOT NULL,
  start_date DATE NOT NULL,
  maturity_date DATE NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Facility PRIMARY KEY (Facility_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Facility_Customer_ID FOREIGN KEY (Customer_ID) REFERENCES Customer (Customer_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Loan (
  Loan_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  loan_amount DECIMAL(18,2) NOT NULL,
  interest_rate DECIMAL(9,4) NOT NULL,
  disbursement_date DATE NOT NULL,
  loan_status VARCHAR(30) NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Loan PRIMARY KEY (Loan_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Loan_Facility_ID FOREIGN KEY (Facility_ID) REFERENCES Facility (Facility_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Collateral (
  Collateral_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  collateral_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  collateral_value DECIMAL(18,2) NOT NULL,
  valuation_date DATE NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Collateral PRIMARY KEY (Collateral_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Facility_Collateral (
  Facility_Collateral_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Collateral_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  lien_order INTEGER NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Facility_Collateral PRIMARY KEY (Facility_Collateral_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Facility_Collateral_Facility_ID FOREIGN KEY (Facility_ID) REFERENCES Facility (Facility_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Facility_Collateral_Collateral_ID FOREIGN KEY (Collateral_ID) REFERENCES Collateral (Collateral_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Facility_Guarantor (
  Facility_Guarantor_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Guarantor_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Facility_Guarantor PRIMARY KEY (Facility_Guarantor_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Facility_Guarantor_Facility_ID FOREIGN KEY (Facility_ID) REFERENCES Facility (Facility_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Facility_Guarantor_Guarantor_ID FOREIGN KEY (Guarantor_ID) REFERENCES Guarantor (Guarantor_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Provision (
  Provision_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  provision_date DATE NOT NULL,
  stage VARCHAR(30) NOT NULL,
  provision_amount DECIMAL(18,2) NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Provision PRIMARY KEY (Provision_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Provision_Facility_ID FOREIGN KEY (Facility_ID) REFERENCES Facility (Facility_ID)
);

Business Requirements Document (BRD)
Loan Banking Data Mart & Reporting Initiative
Version 1.0
1. Document Purpose
This BRD defines the business requirements for designing and implementing a loan banking data mart and reporting solution. The document consolidates business needs captured in the provided workbook and translates them into a formal narrative format suitable for project delivery, stakeholder sign-off, and implementation planning.
2. Business Background
Loan banking functions typically operate across multiple systems for origination, servicing, collections, collateral management, and finance. Fragmented data creates delays in reporting, inconsistent metrics, and manual reconciliation effort. A unified analytical layer is required to improve visibility across the lending lifecycle.
3. Business Objectives
The target state is to create a governed and scalable data foundation that enables accurate portfolio reporting, risk monitoring, collections prioritization, recovery tracking, and management decision-making.
4. Scope
In scope are loan accounts, customer relationships, balances, repayment schedules, delinquency indicators, collateral details, guarantor information, provisions, recoveries, and associated reference data. Out of scope are changes to core transaction processing systems and front-office workflow redesign unless specifically approved.
5. Stakeholders
Primary stakeholders include Lending Operations, Credit Risk, Collections, Finance, Regulatory Reporting, Data Engineering, BI/Analytics teams, Compliance, and Technology Support.

business_rules: A customer is the primary party against whom credit exposure is established.
A facility is the sanctioned credit arrangement and parent container for loans or drawdowns.
A loan is created under a facility and represents an individual funded exposure.
Facility collateral is the bridge concept used to associate facilities with collateral items and capture lien order.
Provision is recorded for a facility at a reporting date and includes stage-based provision information.
Stage distinguishes performing, underperforming, and credit-impaired exposures for loss estimation.
A facility may have one or more collateral items and one or more guarantors.
Historical records are retained according to the applicable retention policy.
conceptual.conceptual_summary: The model centers on Customer, Facility, and Loan to represent the lending hierarchy. Risk mitigation is represented through Collateral, Facility Collateral, and Guarantor. Accounting and risk reporting are supported through Provision and Stage. The relationships reflect how credit is established, disbursed, secured, and provisioned across the lending lifecycle.
conceptual.diagram_description: Customer sits at the top of the model and connects to Facility in a one-to-many relationship. Facility connects to Loan in a one-to-many relationship. Facility also connects to Facility Collateral, which bridges to Collateral to represent secured arrangements and lien order. Facility connects to Guarantor in a many-to-many relationship to represent third-party credit support. Facility connects to Provision in a one-to-many relationship to capture period-based loss allowance reporting.

normalization_notes: All conceptual entities were converted to logical tables using surrogate numeric primary keys.
The M:N relationship between Facility and Guarantor was resolved using the associative table Facility_Guarantor.
Facility Collateral was modeled as an associative table between Facility and Collateral and retains lien_order as a relationship attribute.
Business-relevant attributes from the conceptual model were preserved and represented as generic logical data types only.
Foreign keys enforce referential integrity between Customer, Facility, Loan, Collateral, Guarantor, and Provision.
Provision is maintained at the facility level and includes reporting date and stage for credit loss reporting.
No physical design elements such as indexes, partitions, or storage definitions are included.

CREATE TABLE Customer (
  Customer_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  customer_name VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  customer_type VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
  date_of_birth DATE NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Customer PRIMARY KEY (Customer_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Facility (
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Customer_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  facility_type VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
  sanctioned_amount DECIMAL(18,2) NOT NULL,
  start_date DATE NOT NULL,
  maturity_date DATE NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Facility PRIMARY KEY (Facility_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Facility_Customer_ID FOREIGN KEY (Customer_ID) REFERENCES Customer (Customer_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Loan (
  Loan_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  loan_amount DECIMAL(18,2) NOT NULL,
  interest_rate DECIMAL(9,4) NOT NULL,
  disbursement_date DATE NOT NULL,
  loan_status VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Loan PRIMARY KEY (Loan_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Loan_Facility_ID FOREIGN KEY (Facility_ID) REFERENCES Facility (Facility_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Collateral (
  Collateral_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  collateral_type VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
  collateral_value DECIMAL(18,2) NOT NULL,
  valuation_date DATE NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Collateral PRIMARY KEY (Collateral_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Facility_Collateral (
  Facility_Collateral_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Collateral_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  lien_order INTEGER NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Facility_Collateral PRIMARY KEY (Facility_Collateral_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Facility_Collateral_Facility_ID FOREIGN KEY (Facility_ID) REFERENCES Facility (Facility_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Facility_Collateral_Collateral_ID FOREIGN KEY (Collateral_ID) REFERENCES Collateral (Collateral_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Facility_Guarantor (
  Facility_Guarantor_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Guarantor_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Facility_Guarantor PRIMARY KEY (Facility_Guarantor_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Facility_Guarantor_Facility_ID FOREIGN KEY (Facility_ID) REFERENCES Facility (Facility_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Facility_Guarantor_Guarantor_ID FOREIGN KEY (Guarantor_ID) REFERENCES Guarantor (Guarantor_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Provision (
  Provision_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  provision_date DATE NOT NULL,
  stage VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  provision_amount DECIMAL(18,2) NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Provision PRIMARY KEY (Provision_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Provision_Facility_ID FOREIGN KEY (Facility_ID) REFERENCES Facility (Facility_ID)
);

BRD Document Content:
Business Requirements Document (BRD)
Loan Banking Data Mart & Reporting Initiative
Version 1.0
1. Document Purpose
This BRD defines the business requirements for designing and implementing a loan banking data mart and reporting solution. The document consolidates business needs captured in the provided workbook and translates them into a formal narrative format suitable for project delivery, stakeholder sign-off, and implementation planning.
2. Business Background
Loan banking functions typically operate across multiple systems for origination, servicing, collections, collateral management, and finance. Fragmented data creates delays in reporting, inconsistent metrics, and manual reconciliation effort. A unified analytical layer is required to improve visibility across the lending lifecycle.
3. Business Objectives
The target state is to create a governed and scalable data foundation that enables accurate portfolio reporting, risk monitoring, collections prioritization, recovery tracking, and management decision-making.
4. Scope
In scope are loan accounts, customer relationships, balances, repayment schedules, delinquency indicators, collateral details, guarantor information, provisions, recoveries, and associated reference data. Out of scope are changes to core transaction processing systems and front-office workflow redesign unless specifically approved.
5. Stakeholders
Primary stakeholders include Lending Operations, Credit Risk, Collections, Finance, Regulatory Reporting, Data Engineering, BI/Analytics teams, Compliance, and Technology Support.
6. Current State Challenges
Current reporting processes rely on multiple extracts and manual spreadsheets. Definitions of KPIs may vary across teams. Data lineage is unclear for several metrics. Turnaround time for ad hoc analysis is high, and reconciliation cycles delay management reporting.
7. Future State Requirements
The future solution shall provide curated data marts, standardized calculations, reusable dimensions, documented business rules, historical trending capability, and controlled access for business consumers.
8. Functional Requirements
System shall ingest data from all approved source tables identified in the workbook.
System shall standardize business keys for customer, loan, facility, collateral, and branch entities.
System shall apply transformation logic and derivations captured in column logic/reference sheets.
System shall generate subject-area data marts for portfolio, delinquency, collateral, provisioning, and recovery reporting.
System shall maintain historical snapshots where periodic trend analysis is required.
System shall support drill-down from summary KPIs to underlying account-level records.
System shall provide metadata definitions for each published attribute.
System shall support scheduled refresh cycles and controlled reruns.
9. Reporting Requirements
Outstanding portfolio by product, geography, segment, and branch.
Past-due balances by aging bucket and movement trends.
High-risk exposure and concentration dashboards.
Collateral coverage ratios and unsecured exposure views.
Provision balances and movement analysis.
Recovery collections by channel, period, and product.
Executive scorecards with monthly and quarterly trends.
10. Data Requirements
The workbook references multiple sheets containing business summaries, datamart overviews, column logic, and loan table column references. These inputs shall be treated as authoritative sources for data element definitions, source mappings, and calculation logic during design and build phases.
11. Business Rules
Latest status-based attributes shall use the most recent effective date.
Amounts shall be aggregated at the agreed reporting grain.
Null or invalid keys shall be quarantined for remediation.
Derived flags and buckets shall follow approved business definitions.
Historical records shall be retained according to retention policy.
12. Non-Functional Requirements
Performance: Standard reports should meet agreed SLA timings.
Scalability: Architecture should support growth in loan volume and new products.
Security: Role-based access control and masking for sensitive fields.
Auditability: Full lineage from report field to source system.
Availability: Data marts available as per business calendar.
Quality: Reconciliation, completeness, duplicate, and validity checks.
13. Assumptions & Dependencies
Source systems will provide timely extracts. Business SMEs will validate definitions and exceptions. Required infrastructure, connectivity, and access approvals will be available within project timelines.
14. Risks
Risks include source data quality issues, evolving regulatory definitions, delayed sign-offs, and upstream schema changes. These shall be managed through governance and change control.
15. Acceptance Criteria
Approved in-scope data sources are integrated.
KPIs reconcile with legacy reports within agreed tolerance.
Business users sign off on critical reports and definitions.
Security controls are tested and approved.
Operational support documentation is completed.

CREATE TABLE Customer (

BRD Document Content:
Business Requirements Document (BRD)
Loan Banking Data Mart & Reporting Initiative
Version 1.0
1. Document Purpose
This BRD defines the business requirements for designing and implementing a loan banking data mart and reporting solution. The document consolidates business needs captured in the provided workbook and translates them into a formal narrative format suitable for project delivery, stakeholder sign-off, and implementation planning.
2. Business Background
Loan banking functions typically operate across multiple systems for origination, servicing, collections, collateral management, and finance. Fragmented data creates delays in reporting, inconsistent metrics, and manual reconciliation effort. A unified analytical layer is required to improve visibility across the lending lifecycle.
3. Business Objectives
The target state is to create a governed and scalable data foundation that enables accurate portfolio reporting, risk monitoring, collections prioritization, recovery tracking, and management decision-making.
4. Scope
In scope are loan accounts, customer relationships, balances, repayment schedules, delinquency indicators, collateral details, guarantor information, provisions, recoveries, and associated reference data. Out of scope are changes to core transaction processing systems and front-office workflow redesign unless specifically approved.
5. Stakeholders
Primary stakeholders include Lending Operations, Credit Risk, Collections, Finance, Regulatory Reporting, Data Engineering, BI/Analytics teams, Compliance, and Technology Support.
6. Current State Challenges
Current reporting processes rely on multiple extracts and manual spreadsheets. Definitions of KPIs may vary across teams. Data lineage is unclear for several metrics. Turnaround time for ad hoc analysis is high, and reconciliation cycles delay management reporting.
7. Future State Requirements
The future solution shall provide curated data marts, standardized calculations, reusable dimensions, documented business rules, historical trending capability, and controlled access for business consumers.
8. Functional Requirements
System shall ingest data from all approved source tables identified in the workbook.
System shall standardize business keys for customer, loan, facility, collateral, and branch entities.
System shall apply transformation logic and derivations captured in column logic/reference sheets.
System shall generate subject-area data marts for portfolio, delinquency, collateral, provisioning, and recovery reporting.
System shall maintain historical snapshots where periodic trend analysis is required.
System shall support drill-down from summary KPIs to underlying account-level records.
System shall provide metadata definitions for each published attribute.
System shall support scheduled refresh cycles and controlled reruns.
9. Reporting Requirements
Outstanding portfolio by product, geography, segment, and branch.
Past-due balances by aging bucket and movement trends.
High-risk exposure and concentration dashboards.
Collateral coverage ratios and unsecured exposure views.
Provision balances and movement analysis.
Recovery collections by channel, period, and product.
Executive scorecards with monthly and quarterly trends.
10. Data Requirements
The workbook references multiple sheets containing business summaries, datamart overviews, column logic, and loan table column references. These inputs shall be treated as authoritative sources for data element definitions, source mappings, and calculation logic during design and build phases.
11. Business Rules
Latest status-based attributes shall use the most recent effective date.
Amounts shall be aggregated at the agreed reporting grain.
Null or invalid keys shall be quarantined for remediation.
Derived flags and buckets shall follow approved business definitions.
Historical records shall be retained according to retention policy.
12. Non-Functional Requirements
Performance: Standard reports should meet agreed SLA timings.
Scalability: Architecture should support growth in loan volume and new products.
Security: Role-based access control and masking for sensitive fields.
Auditability: Full lineage from report field to source system.
Availability: Data marts available as per business calendar.
Quality: Reconciliation, completeness, duplicate, and validity checks.
13. Assumptions & Dependencies
Source systems will provide timely extracts. Business SMEs will validate definitions and exceptions. Required infrastructure, connectivity, and access approvals will be available within project timelines.
14. Risks
Risks include source data quality issues, evolving regulatory definitions, delayed sign-offs, and upstream schema changes. These shall be managed through governance and change control.
15. Acceptance Criteria
Approved in-scope data sources are integrated.
KPIs reconcile with legacy reports within agreed tolerance.
Business users sign off on critical reports and definitions.
Security controls are tested and approved.
Operational support documentation is completed.
Conceptual draft generated. Review the conceptual ER and use the same /orchestrate endpoint with artifact_id to update relationships or send 'approve' to continue.
Conceptual draft approved and used to generate logical and physical models.

conceptual.rag_context_used:
Business concept: Customer. Canonical table: CUSTOMER. Definition: A borrower or counterparty who receives credit from the institution. Business purpose: Represents the primary party against whom credit exposure is established. Notes: One customer can have one or more facilities.
Business concept: Facility. Canonical table: FACILITY. Definition: A sanctioned credit arrangement approved for a customer, such as a term loan, overdraft, or working capital line. Business purpose: Acts as the contractual credit agreement and parent container for one or more loans or drawdowns. Notes: Central entity in the loan model.
Business concept: Loan. Canonical table: LOAN. Definition: An individual drawdown or funded exposure created under a facility. Business purpose: Tracks the specific credit amount disbursed, pricing, and lifecycle status. Notes: A facility can generate one or more loans.
Business concept: Collateral. Canonical table: COLLATERAL. Definition: An asset pledged as security against credit exposure. Business purpose: Mitigates credit risk by providing secondary repayment support. Notes: Examples include property, receivables, inventory, vehicles, and deposits.
Business concept: Facility Collateral. Canonical table: FACILITY_COLLATERAL. Definition: The bridge entity linking a facility to one or more collateral records. Business purpose: Resolves the many-to-many style business association between secured facilities and collateral items. Notes: Also stores lien order in this model.
Business concept: Guarantor. Canonical table: GUARANTOR. Definition: A third party that legally supports repayment if the borrower fails to perform. Business purpose: Provides additional credit enhancement beyond the borrower and collateral. Notes: Can be an individual or a corporate guarantor.
Business concept: Provision. Canonical table: PROVISION. Definition: A period-end expected or recognized credit loss amount for a facility. Business purpose: Supports accounting and risk reporting by recording loss allowance over time. Notes: Includes stage-based provision amounts in this model.
Business concept: Stage. Canonical table: PROVISION. Definition: A credit impairment classification used for provisioning. Business purpose: Distinguishes performing, underperforming, and credit-impaired exposures for loss estimation. Notes: This synthetic model includes Stage 1, Stage 2, and Stage 3 values.
Business concept: Sanctioned Amount. Canonical table: FACILITY. Definition: The approved credit limit or facility size granted to the customer. Business purpose: Defines the maximum contractual exposure approved under the facility. Notes: A loan amount may be lower than the sanctioned amount.
Table summary: CUSTOMER represents Customer. Primary key: customer_id. Business attributes: customer_name means display name of the borrower or counterparty; customer_type means segment of the customer such as Retail, SME, or Corporate; date_of_birth means date of birth of the customer. Examples: customer_id example C00001; customer_name example Aarav Sharma; customer_type example Retail.
Table summary: FACILITY represents Facility. Related business terms: Sanctioned Amount. Primary key: facility_id. Foreign keys: customer_id references CUSTOMER.customer_id. Business attributes: facility_type means type of sanctioned credit arrangement; sanctioned_amount means approved amount or limit for the facility; start_date means facility effective or start date; maturity_date means scheduled end or maturity date of the facility. Examples: facility_id example F00001; customer_id example C00001; facility_type example Term Loan.
Table summary: LOAN represents Loan. Primary key: loan_id. Foreign keys: facility_id references FACILITY.facility_id. Business attributes: loan_amount means amount disbursed for the individual loan; interest_rate means contractual pricing rate associated with the loan; disbursement_date means date on which the loan amount was funded or made available; loan_status means lifecycle status of the loan. Examples: loan_id example L00001; facility_id example F00001; loan_amount example 650000.
Table summary: COLLATERAL represents Collateral. Primary key: collateral_id. Business attributes: collateral_type means type of pledged security; collateral_value means assessed value of the collateral at valuation date; valuation_date means date on which the collateral was valued. Examples: collateral_id example CL00001; collateral_type example Property; collateral_value example 1500000.
Table summary: FACILITY_COLLATERAL represents Facility Collateral. Primary key: facility_collateral_id. Foreign keys: facility_id references FACILITY.facility_id; collateral_id references COLLATERAL.collateral_id. Business attributes: lien_order means priority of the institution's charge over the collateral. Examples: facility_collateral_id example FC00001; facility_id example F00001; collateral_id example CL00001.
Table summary: GUARANTOR represents Guarantor. Primary key: guarantor_id. Business attributes: guarantor_name means name of the guarantor party; guarantor_type means type of guarantor such as Individual or Corporate. Examples: guarantor_id example G00001; guarantor_name example Priya Mehta; guarantor_type example Individual.
Table summary: PROVISION represents Provision. Related business terms: Stage. Primary key: provision_id. Foreign keys: facility_id references FACILITY.facility_id. Business attributes: provision_date means reporting date for the provision amount; stage means impairment stage associated with the provision record; provision_amount means recognized or expected credit loss amount for the facility at provision date. Examples: provision_id example P000001; facility_id example F00001; provision_date example 2025-12-31.

conceptual.conceptual_summary: The model centers on Customer, Facility, and Loan as the core lending hierarchy, with collateral support managed through Facility Collateral, and risk/credit loss reporting represented by Provision. Guarantor is included as an additional credit enhancement party. The model supports business views of sanctioned credit, funded exposure, secured lending, and loss allowance across the lending lifecycle.
conceptual.diagram_description: Customer sits at the top of the lending hierarchy and connects to one or more Facilities. Each Facility can generate one or more Loans and can also have one or more Provision records over time. Facility Collateral bridges the many-to-many business association between Facility and Collateral, capturing lien order. Guarantor stands as a separate supporting party associated to the lending context through the overall model scope, while remaining a distinct concept in the glossary.

Guarantor is modeled as a separate entity linked to Customer, consistent with the approved conceptual relationship.
Identifier columns are numeric surrogate keys to satisfy logical model requirements and maintain consistent PK/FK naming.

Business Requirements Document (BRD)
Loan Banking Data Mart & Reporting Initiative
Version 1.0
1. Document Purpose
This BRD defines the business requirements for designing and implementing a loan banking data mart and reporting solution. The document consolidates business needs captured in the provided workbook and translates them into a formal narrative format suitable for project delivery, stakeholder sign-off, and implementation planning.
2. Business Background
Loan banking functions typically operate across multiple systems for origination, servicing, collections, collateral management, and finance. Fragmented data creates delays in reporting, inconsistent metrics, and manual reconciliation effort. A unified analytical layer is required to improve visibility across the lending lifecycle.
3. Business Objectives
The target state is to create a governed and scalable data foundation that enables accurate portfolio reporting, risk monitoring, collections prioritization, recovery tracking, and management decision-making.
4. Scope
In scope are loan accounts, customer relationships, balances, repayment schedules, delinquency indicators, collateral details, guarantor information, provisions, recoveries, and associated reference data. Out of scope are changes to core transaction processing systems and front-office workflow redesign unless specifically approved.
5. Stakeholders
Primary stakeholders include Lending Operations, Credit Risk, Collections, Finance, Regulatory Reporting, Data Engineering, BI/Analytics teams, Compliance, and Technology Support.
6. Current State Challenges
Current reporting processes rely on multiple extracts and manual spreadsheets. Definitions of KPIs may vary across teams. Data lineage is unclear for several metrics. Turnaround time for ad hoc analysis is high, and reconciliation cycles delay management reporting.
7. Future State Requirements
The future solution shall provide curated data marts, standardized calculations, reusable dimensions, documented business rules, historical trending capability, and controlled access for business consumers.
8. Functional Requirements
System shall ingest data from all approved source tables identified in the workbook.
System shall standardize business keys for customer, loan, facility, collateral, and branch entities.
System shall apply transformation logic and derivations captured in column logic/reference sheets.
System shall generate subject-area data marts for portfolio, delinquency, collateral, provisioning, and recovery reporting.
System shall maintain historical snapshots where periodic trend analysis is required.
System shall support drill-down from summary KPIs to underlying account-level records.
System shall provide metadata definitions for each published attribute.
System shall support scheduled refresh cycles and controlled reruns.
9. Reporting Requirements
Outstanding portfolio by product, geography, segment, and branch.
Past-due balances by aging bucket and movement trends.
High-risk exposure and concentration dashboards.
Collateral coverage ratios and unsecured exposure views.
Provision balances and movement analysis.
Recovery collections by channel, period, and product.
Executive scorecards with monthly and quarterly trends.
10. Data Requirements
The workbook references multiple sheets containing business summaries, datamart overviews, column logic, and loan table column references. These inputs shall be treated as authoritative sources for data element definitions, source mappings, and calculation logic during design and build phases.
11. Business Rules
Latest status-based attributes shall use the most recent effective date.
Amounts shall be aggregated at the agreed reporting grain.
Null or invalid keys shall be quarantined for remediation.
Derived flags and buckets shall follow approved business definitions.
Historical records shall be retained according to retention policy.
12. Non-Functional Requirements
Performance: Standard reports should meet agreed SLA timings.
Scalability: Architecture should support growth in loan volume and new products.
Security: Role-based access control and masking for sensitive fields.
Auditability: Full lineage from report field to source system.
Availability: Data marts available as per business calendar.
Quality: Reconciliation, completeness, duplicate, and validity checks.
13. Assumptions & Dependencies
Source systems will provide timely extracts. Business SMEs will validate definitions and exceptions. Required infrastructure, connectivity, and access approvals will be available within project timelines.
14. Risks
Risks include source data quality issues, evolving regulatory definitions, delayed sign-offs, and upstream schema changes. These shall be managed through governance and change control.
15. Acceptance Criteria
Approved in-scope data sources are integrated.
KPIs reconcile with legacy reports within agreed tolerance.
Business users sign off on critical reports and definitions.
Security controls are tested and approved.
Operational support documentation is completed.

model_json.rag_context_used:
Business concept: Customer. Canonical table: CUSTOMER. Definition: A borrower or counterparty who receives credit from the institution. Business purpose: Represents the primary party against whom credit exposure is established. Notes: One customer can have one or more facilities.
Business concept: Facility. Canonical table: FACILITY. Definition: A sanctioned credit arrangement approved for a customer, such as a term loan, overdraft, or working capital line. Business purpose: Acts as the contractual credit agreement and parent container for one or more loans or drawdowns. Notes: Central entity in the loan model.
Business concept: Loan. Canonical table: LOAN. Definition: An individual drawdown or funded exposure created under a facility. Business purpose: Tracks the specific credit amount disbursed, pricing, and lifecycle status. Notes: A facility can generate one or more loans.
Business concept: Collateral. Canonical table: COLLATERAL. Definition: An asset pledged as security against credit exposure. Business purpose: Mitigates credit risk by providing secondary repayment support. Notes: Examples include property, receivables, inventory, vehicles, and deposits.
Business concept: Facility Collateral. Canonical table: FACILITY_COLLATERAL. Definition: The bridge entity linking a facility to one or more collateral records. Business purpose: Resolves the many-to-many style business association between secured facilities and collateral items. Notes: Also stores lien order in this model.
Business concept: Guarantor. Canonical table: GUARANTOR. Definition: A third party that legally supports repayment if the borrower fails to perform. Business purpose: Provides additional credit enhancement beyond the borrower and collateral. Notes: Can be an individual or a corporate guarantor.
Business concept: Provision. Canonical table: PROVISION. Definition: A period-end expected or recognized credit loss amount for a facility. Business purpose: Supports accounting and risk reporting by recording loss allowance over time. Notes: Includes stage-based provision amounts in this model.
Business concept: Stage. Canonical table: PROVISION. Definition: A credit impairment classification used for provisioning. Business purpose: Distinguishes performing, underperforming, and credit-impaired exposures for loss estimation. Notes: This synthetic model includes Stage 1, Stage 2, and Stage 3 values.
Business concept: Sanctioned Amount. Canonical table: FACILITY. Definition: The approved credit limit or facility size granted to the customer. Business purpose: Defines the maximum contractual exposure approved under the facility. Notes: A loan amount may be lower than the sanctioned amount.
Table summary: CUSTOMER represents Customer. Primary key: customer_id. Business attributes: customer_name means display name of the borrower or counterparty; customer_type means segment of the customer such as Retail, SME, or Corporate; date_of_birth means date of birth of the customer. Examples: customer_id example C00001; customer_name example Aarav Sharma; customer_type example Retail.
Table summary: FACILITY represents Facility. Related business terms: Sanctioned Amount. Primary key: facility_id. Foreign keys: customer_id references CUSTOMER.customer_id. Business attributes: facility_type means type of sanctioned credit arrangement; sanctioned_amount means approved amount or limit for the facility; start_date means facility effective or start date; maturity_date means scheduled end or maturity date of the facility. Examples: facility_id example F00001; customer_id example C00001; facility_type example Term Loan.
Table summary: LOAN represents Loan. Primary key: loan_id. Foreign keys: facility_id references FACILITY.facility_id. Business attributes: loan_amount means amount disbursed for the individual loan; interest_rate means contractual pricing rate associated with the loan; disbursement_date means date on which the loan amount was funded or made available; loan_status means lifecycle status of the loan. Examples: loan_id example L00001; facility_id example F00001; loan_amount example 650000.
Table summary: COLLATERAL represents Collateral. Primary key: collateral_id. Business attributes: collateral_type means type of pledged security; collateral_value means assessed value of the collateral at valuation date; valuation_date means date on which the collateral was valued. Examples: collateral_id example CL00001; collateral_type example Property; collateral_value example 1500000.
Table summary: FACILITY_COLLATERAL represents Facility Collateral. Primary key: facility_collateral_id. Foreign keys: facility_id references FACILITY.facility_id; collateral_id references COLLATERAL.collateral_id. Business attributes: lien_order means priority of the institution's charge over the collateral. Examples: facility_collateral_id example FC00001; facility_id example F00001; collateral_id example CL00001.
Table summary: GUARANTOR represents Guarantor. Primary key: guarantor_id. Business attributes: guarantor_name means name of the guarantor party; guarantor_type means type of guarantor such as Individual or Corporate. Examples: guarantor_id example G00001; guarantor_name example Priya Mehta; guarantor_type example Individual.
Table summary: PROVISION represents Provision. Related business terms: Stage. Primary key: provision_id. Foreign keys: facility_id references FACILITY.facility_id. Business attributes: provision_date means reporting date for the provision amount; stage means impairment stage associated with the provision record; provision_amount means recognized or expected credit loss amount for the facility at provision date. Examples: provision_id example P000001; facility_id example F00001; provision_date example 2025-12-31.

model_json.conceptual_summary: The model centers on Customer, Facility, and Loan as the core lending hierarchy, with collateral support managed through Facility Collateral, and risk/credit loss reporting represented by Provision. Guarantor is included as an additional credit enhancement party. The model supports business views of sanctioned credit, funded exposure, secured lending, and loss allowance across the lending lifecycle.
model_json.diagram_description: Customer sits at the top of the lending hierarchy and connects to one or more Facilities. Each Facility can generate one or more Loans and can also have one or more Provision records over time. Facility Collateral bridges the many-to-many business association between Facility and Collateral, capturing lien order. Guarantor stands as a separate supporting party associated to the lending context through the overall model scope, while remaining a distinct concept in the glossary.

CREATE TABLE Customer (
  Customer_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  customer_name VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,
  customer_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  date_of_birth DATE NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Customer PRIMARY KEY (Customer_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Facility (
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Customer_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  facility_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  sanctioned_amount DECIMAL(18,2) NOT NULL,
  start_date DATE NOT NULL,
  maturity_date DATE NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Facility PRIMARY KEY (Facility_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Facility_Customer_ID FOREIGN KEY (Customer_ID) REFERENCES Customer (Customer_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Loan (
  Loan_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  loan_amount DECIMAL(18,2) NOT NULL,
  interest_rate DECIMAL(9,6) NOT NULL,
  disbursement_date DATE NOT NULL,
  loan_status VARCHAR(30) NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Loan PRIMARY KEY (Loan_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Loan_Facility_ID FOREIGN KEY (Facility_ID) REFERENCES Facility (Facility_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Collateral (
  Collateral_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  collateral_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  collateral_value DECIMAL(18,2) NOT NULL,
  valuation_date DATE NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Collateral PRIMARY KEY (Collateral_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Facility_Collateral (
  Facility_Collateral_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Collateral_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  lien_order INTEGER NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Facility_Collateral PRIMARY KEY (Facility_Collateral_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Facility_Collateral_Facility_ID FOREIGN KEY (Facility_ID) REFERENCES Facility (Facility_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Facility_Collateral_Collateral_ID FOREIGN KEY (Collateral_ID) REFERENCES Collateral (Collateral_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Guarantor (
  Guarantor_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  customer_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  guarantor_name VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,
  guarantor_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Guarantor PRIMARY KEY (Guarantor_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Guarantor_customer_ID FOREIGN KEY (customer_ID) REFERENCES Customer (Customer_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE Provision (
  Provision_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Facility_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  provision_date DATE NOT NULL,
  stage VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,
  provision_amount DECIMAL(18,2) NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_Provision PRIMARY KEY (Provision_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_Provision_Facility_ID FOREIGN KEY (Facility_ID) REFERENCES Facility (Facility_ID)
);

Business concept: Loan. Canonical table: LOAN. Definition: An individual drawdown or funded exposure created under a facility. Business purpose: Tracks the specific credit amount disbursed, pricing, and lifecycle status. Notes: A facility can generate one or more loans.
Business concept: Collateral. Canonical table: COLLATERAL. Definition: An asset pledged as security against credit exposure. Business purpose: Mitigates credit risk by providing secondary repayment support. Notes: Examples include property, receivables, inventory, vehicles, and deposits.
Business concept: Facility Collateral. Canonical table: FACILITY_COLLATERAL. Definition: The bridge entity linking a facility to one or more collateral records. Business purpose: Resolves the many-to-many style business association between secured facilities and collateral items. Notes: Also stores lien order in this model.
Business concept: Guarantor. Canonical table: GUARANTOR. Definition: A third party that legally supports repayment if the borrower fails to perform. Business purpose: Provides additional credit enhancement beyond the borrower and collateral. Notes: Can be an individual or a corporate guarantor.

Business purpose: Distinguishes performing, underperforming, and credit-impaired exposures for loss estimation. Notes: This synthetic model includes Stage 1, Stage 2, and Stage 3 values.
Business concept: Sanctioned Amount. Canonical table: FACILITY. Definition: The approved credit limit or facility size granted to the customer. Business purpose: Defines the maximum contractual exposure approved under the facility. Notes: A loan amount may be lower than the sanctioned amount.

Business attributes: facility_type means type of sanctioned credit arrangement; sanctioned_amount means approved amount or limit for the facility; start_date means facility effective or start date; maturity_date means scheduled end or maturity date of the facility. Examples: facility_id example F00001; customer_id example C00001; facility_type example Term Loan.
Table summary: LOAN represents Loan. Primary key: loan_id. Foreign keys: facility_id references FACILITY.facility_id. Loan Banking Data Mart & Reporting Initiative
Version 1.0
1. Document Purpose
This BRD defines the business requirements for designing and implementing a loan banking data mart and reporting solution. The document consolidates business needs captured in the provided workbook and translates them into a formal narrative format suitable for project delivery, stakeholder sign-off, and implementation planning.
2. Business Background
Loan banking functions typically operate across multiple systems for origination, servicing, collections, collateral management, and finance. Fragmented data creates delays in reporting, inconsistent metrics, and manual reconciliation effort. A unified analytical layer is required to improve visibility across the lending lifecycle.
3. Business Objectives
The target state is to create a governed and scalable data foundation that enables accurate portfolio reporting, risk monitoring, collections prioritization, recovery tracking, and management decision-making.
4. Scope
In scope are loan accounts, customer relationships, balances, repayment schedules, delinquency indicators, collateral details, guarantor information, provisions, recoveries, and associated reference data. Out of scope are changes to core transaction processing systems and front-office workflow redesign unless specifically approved.
5. Stakeholders
Primary stakeholders include Lending Operations, Credit Risk, Collections, Finance, Regulatory Reporting, Data Engineering, BI/Analytics teams, Compliance, and Technology Support.

Current reporting processes rely on multiple extracts and manual spreadsheets. Definitions of KPIs may vary across teams. Data lineage is unclear for several metrics. Turnaround time for ad hoc analysis is high, and reconciliation cycles delay management reporting.
7. Future State Requirements
The future solution shall provide curated data marts, standardized calculations, reusable dimensions, documented business rules, historical trending capability, and controlled access for business consumers.
8. Functional Requirements
System shall ingest data from all approved source tables identified in the workbook.
System shall standardize business keys for customer, loan, facility, collateral, and branch entities.

System shall generate subject-area data marts for portfolio, delinquency, collateral, provisioning, and recovery reporting.
